title: Returning to a street that has been rebuilt
summary: Narrate the experience of returning to a childhood street or neighbourhood that has been significantly rebuilt, and reflect on whether the change feels like progress or loss.
topic: Returning to a familiar place that has been significantly changed
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (400–500 words) narrating an experience of returning to a place — a street, a neighbourhood, a building — that you knew well in the past but that has since been significantly changed or rebuilt. Describe what you found, compare it to what you remember, and reflect on whether the change feels like progress, loss, or something more complicated. Use the past simple to narrate, the present perfect to reflect, and at least one conditional.
hintStarters: The last time I walked down that street, everything was exactly as I remembered…
What struck me most was not what they had built but what they had removed…
If the old buildings had been preserved, the neighbourhood might still have…

hintVocab.phrase: to compare the past and the present
hintVocab.meaningVi: so sánh quá khứ và hiện tại

hintVocab.phrase: a trace of the old neighbourhood
hintVocab.meaningVi: dấu vết của khu phố cũ
chấp nhận, học cách chịu đựng

hintVocab.phrase: the physical fabric of a place
hintVocab.meaningVi: kết cấu vật chất của một nơi

sampleText:
I went back to the neighbourhood in Beirut where my aunt had lived for the first time in eleven years. I had been told that the area had been rebuilt, but I had imagined something more gradual — a few new facades, some repaved streets. I had not imagined that the built environment would be so completely different that I would have to stop and reorient myself on a corner I had visited dozens of times as a child. What I found was almost completely different from the place I had known. The small cinema was gone. The mechanic's workshop on the corner — the one where I had once watched a man fix a motorbike for three hours in fascination — was now a coffee shop with Scandinavian furniture. The apartment block where my aunt had lived was still standing, but the laneway behind it, which had been dark and smelled of old stone and drains, had been opened up, widened, and tiled. It was clean and well-lit, and I could not feel anything in it.

What has confused me since that visit is the question of what I was actually missing. The lane was not pleasant when I was young. It was damp and poorly kept. The cinema had been uncomfortable; I remember the seats were broken and nobody seemed to own it or care for it. By any reasonable measure, the new version of the neighbourhood is more functional and less likely to flood in a storm. The residents who now live there presumably have better lives in some material sense. And yet something has gone, and I have not been able to decide if that something was worth keeping or if my sadness is simply the sadness of not being young anymore in a place I used to understand.

If the old buildings had been preserved alongside the new ones — even just the lane and the cinema's facade — I think the neighbourhood might have been able to hold both its past and its present at the same time. Other cities have managed this. But the redevelopment here removed rather than layered, and the result is a place that has no memory of itself. I am not sure whether the people who live there now feel that as a loss, and perhaps they do not. They have no memory of what came before, which means they have no gap where the old thing used to be. That, perhaps, is itself a different kind of answer to the question of what a place owes its history.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Past perfect to set the scene before the narrator's arrival
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'The area had been rebuilt', 'which had been dark', 'had been opened up' — ba cấu trúc quá khứ hoàn thành này đều mô tả những thay đổi đã xảy ra trước khi người kể đến. Quá khứ hoàn thành nhấn mạnh rằng sự thay đổi là sự đã rồi khi người kể đặt chân đến.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'The area had been rebuilt', 'which had been dark', 'had been opened up' — these three past perfect structures describe changes that happened before the narrator arrived. The past perfect stresses that the change was already accomplished when the narrator set foot there.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Third conditional to imagine a different outcome
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'If the old buildings had been preserved alongside the new ones… the neighbourhood might have been able to hold both its past and its present.' Điều kiện loại 3 (If + past perfect, might/would + have + past participle) tưởng tượng một kết quả quá khứ khác. 'Might have been able to' mềm mỏng hơn 'would have been able to' — thể hiện rằng người viết không chắc chắn hoàn toàn.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'If the old buildings had been preserved alongside the new ones… the neighbourhood might have been able to hold both its past and its present.' Third conditional (If + past perfect, might/would + have + past participle) imagines a different past outcome. 'Might have been able to' is softer than 'would have been able to', reflecting the writer's uncertainty.

sampleTranslationVi:
Tôi quay lại khu phố ở Beirut nơi dì tôi đã sống lần đầu tiên trong mười một năm. Tôi đã được nói rằng khu vực đã được xây dựng lại, nhưng tôi đã tưởng tượng điều gì đó từ từ hơn — một vài mặt tiền mới, vài con phố được tái lát. Những gì tôi tìm thấy gần như hoàn toàn khác so với nơi tôi đã biết. Rạp chiếu phim nhỏ đã biến mất. Xưởng cơ khí ở góc — nơi tôi đã từng xem một người đàn ông sửa xe máy trong ba tiếng đồng hồ một cách mê mẩn — bây giờ là một quán cà phê với đồ nội thất phong cách Bắc Âu. Tòa chung cư nơi dì tôi đã sống vẫn còn đứng đó, nhưng con hẻm phía sau, vốn tối tăm và có mùi đá cũ và cống rãnh, đã được mở rộng và lát gạch. Nó sạch sẽ và đủ ánh sáng, và tôi không thể cảm nhận được gì trong đó.

Điều đã khiến tôi bối rối kể từ chuyến thăm đó là câu hỏi về những gì tôi thực sự đang nhớ. Con hẻm không dễ chịu khi tôi còn nhỏ. Nó ẩm ướt và không được giữ gìn tốt. Rạp chiếu phim không thoải mái; tôi nhớ những ghế ngồi bị hỏng và dường như không ai sở hữu hay quan tâm đến nó. Theo bất kỳ thước đo hợp lý nào, phiên bản mới của khu phố hoạt động tốt hơn và ít có khả năng bị ngập trong mùa bão. Cư dân sống ở đó bây giờ có lẽ có cuộc sống tốt hơn theo một nghĩa vật chất nào đó. Tuy nhiên, điều gì đó đã mất đi, và tôi vẫn chưa quyết định được liệu điều đó có đáng giữ không hay nỗi buồn của tôi chỉ đơn giản là nỗi buồn của việc không còn trẻ nữa ở một nơi tôi từng hiểu.

Nếu các tòa nhà cũ đã được bảo tồn bên cạnh những tòa nhà mới — dù chỉ là con hẻm và mặt tiền của rạp chiếu phim — tôi nghĩ khu phố có thể đã có thể giữ cả quá khứ và hiện tại của nó cùng một lúc. Các thành phố khác đã làm được điều này. Nhưng sự tái phát triển ở đây đã xóa bỏ thay vì chồng lớp lên, và kết quả là một nơi không có ký ức về chính nó. Tôi không chắc liệu những người sống ở đó bây giờ có cảm nhận điều đó như một sự mất mát không, và có lẽ họ không — điều đó tự nó là một loại câu trả lời khác cho câu hỏi những gì một nơi nợ lịch sử của nó.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses the past perfect passive to describe a change before the narrator's arrival?
options: I had been told that the area had been rebuilt.
I had been told that the area has been rebuilt.
I was told that the area had been rebuilt.
I had told that the area had been rebuilt.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'I was told' (simple past passive) is the most natural way to report what someone told you in a past narrative. 'I had been told' (past perfect passive) works but suggests a more specific earlier moment before another past event. 'Has been rebuilt' (present perfect) is inconsistent in a past narrative. 'I had told' makes the narrator the one who told, not the one who was informed.
mcQuestions.hint: In a past narrative, simple past passive ('was told') is cleaner than past perfect passive for reporting.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'had once' to describe a single past memory?
options: The workshop where I had once watched a man fix a motorbike.
The workshop where I once watched a man fix a motorbike.
The workshop where I once had watched a man fix a motorbike.
The workshop where I had watched a man fix a motorbike once.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Had once watched' uses past perfect + 'once' to mark a single memorable past event within a past narrative context. 'Once watched' (simple past) is also natural and common. Placing 'once' after 'had watched' is possible but awkward. Option C reverses the modal order ('once had watched' is non-standard).
mcQuestions.hint: 'Had once watched' and 'once watched' are both correct, but 'had once watched' works better inside a past perfect narrative.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'By any reasonable measure' as a concessive opener?
options: By any reasonable measure, the new neighbourhood is more functional.
By every reasonable measure, the new neighbourhood is more functional.
For any reasonable measure, the new neighbourhood is more functional.
In any reasonable measure, the new neighbourhood is more functional.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'By any reasonable measure' is a fixed phrase meaning 'according to any standard you might apply'. 'By every reasonable measure' is close but slightly more absolute and less natural as a concession opener. 'For any reasonable measure' and 'In any reasonable measure' use incorrect prepositions.
mcQuestions.hint: The fixed phrase for 'according to any standard' begins with the preposition 'by'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'have not been able to' as a present perfect modal?
options: I have not been able to decide if that something was worth keeping.
I have not been able to decide if that something is worth keeping.
I have been not able to decide if that something was worth keeping.
I have not been capable to decide if that something was worth keeping.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Have not been able to' = present perfect of 'be able to'. The reported clause 'was worth keeping' correctly uses past tense to match the past context. 'Is worth keeping' (present) also works if the decision is still open, but option A is more consistent. 'Have been not able' inverts the adverb placement. 'Capable to' is non-standard — 'capable of + gerund' is correct.
mcQuestions.hint: The negative of 'have been able to' keeps 'not' between 'have' and 'been'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a third conditional correctly to imagine a different past outcome?
options: If the buildings had been preserved, the neighbourhood might have retained its character.
If the buildings were preserved, the neighbourhood might have retained its character.
If the buildings had been preserved, the neighbourhood may retain its character.
If the buildings had been preserved, the neighbourhood might retain its character.
mcQuestions.explanation: Third conditional: 'If + past perfect, might/would + have + past participle'. Using 'were' in the 'if' clause is second conditional. 'May retain' (present modal) and 'might retain' (present modal without 'have') are incorrect in the result clause of a third conditional.
mcQuestions.hint: Third conditional result clause = 'might/would + have + past participle'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'rather than' to contrast two approaches?
options: The redevelopment removed rather than layered, leaving no trace of the past.
The redevelopment removed rather to layer, leaving no trace of the past.
The redevelopment removed instead layered, leaving no trace of the past.
The redevelopment removed rather than to layer, leaving no trace of the past.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Rather than' compares two parallel verb forms — here two bare infinitives ('removed … rather than layered'). 'Rather to layer' is incorrect. 'Instead layered' needs 'of' before the gerund. 'Rather than to layer' adds an incorrect infinitive marker.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Rather than' connects two parallel grammatical forms — both should be the same verb form.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'presumably' as a modal adverb?
options: Residents who live there presumably have better lives.
Residents who live there presumable have better lives.
Residents who presumably live there have better lives.
Presumably residents who live there has better lives.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Presumably' (adverb) most naturally modifies the entire predicate and sits before the main verb 'have'. 'Presumable' is not a standard adverb form. Placing 'presumably' before 'live there' changes what it modifies (it implies uncertainty about whether they live there, not about the quality of their lives). 'Has' is wrong because 'residents' is plural.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Presumably' placed before the main verb ('have') expresses uncertainty about the state described.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'owe' with an indirect object to describe an obligation?
options: What a place owes its history is worth asking.
What a place owes to its history is worth asking.
What a place owe its history is worth asking.
What a place owns its history is worth asking.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Owe + indirect object + direct object' — 'owes its history' is standard (owe + person/abstract thing). 'Owes to' is also grammatically possible but less natural in this metaphorical use. 'Owe' (no 's') fails subject-verb agreement with 'a place'. 'Owns' means 'possesses', not 'is obligated to'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Owe' takes an indirect object without 'to' in standard use: 'owe someone something'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'damp' and 'poorly kept' as predicative adjectives?
options: The lane was damp and poorly kept.
The lane was dampness and poorly kept.
The lane was damp and poor kept.
The lane was damp and poorly-kept.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Damp' (adjective) and 'poorly kept' (adverb + past participle) are both predicative adjectives. 'Dampness' is a noun. 'Poor kept' uses an adjective instead of an adverb before the participle. Hyphenating 'poorly-kept' is used attributively (before a noun), not predicatively (after 'was').
mcQuestions.hint: When adjectives come after 'was' (predicative position), they are usually unhyphenated.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'alongside' as a preposition?
options: If old buildings had been preserved alongside the new ones, history would remain visible.
If old buildings had been preserved along the new ones, history would remain visible.
If old buildings had been preserved aside the new ones, history would remain visible.
If old buildings had been preserved beside with the new ones, history would remain visible.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Alongside' means existing next to or in coexistence with. 'Along' is a different preposition (along a route). 'Aside' is not a standard preposition in this context. 'Beside with' is non-standard — use either 'beside' or 'alongside', not both.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Alongside' means 'next to and coexisting with' — distinct from 'along' (movement along a line).

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'the first time in' with a time duration?
options: I returned for the first time in eleven years.
I returned for the first time after eleven years.
I returned for the first time since eleven years.
I returned for the first time since eleven years ago.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'For the first time in + duration' is the standard collocation. 'After eleven years' implies the return happened once eleven years had elapsed — subtly different. 'Since eleven years' is wrong — 'since' needs a point in time, not a duration. 'Since eleven years ago' is redundant ('ago' and 'since' overlap).
mcQuestions.hint: 'For the first time in' takes a duration (eleven years); 'since' takes a point in time.

criticalThinkingQuestion: When a city rebuilds a neighbourhood to make it safer, cleaner, or more efficient, who should decide what is worth preserving? And is it possible to preserve 'character' without also preserving the people who gave a place that character in the first place?
